aiml_problems.problemStatement: The task involves analyzing movie reviews from IMDB to predict their sentiment as either positive or negative. This dataset contains 50,000 highly polar movie reviews, balanced equally between 25,000 positive and 25,000 negative reviews, making it suitable for training and evaluating binary classifiers such as those used in natural language processing (NLP) tasks like sentiment analysis.
tasks: Task 1: Data Loading and Text Inspection — load the dataset. Display 5 sample texts per class/label. Report vocabulary size, average token length, class distribution, and language distribution if multilingual. Identify any HTML artefacts, special characters, or encoding issues.

Task 2: Text Preprocessing — clean text (lowercase, remove punctuation/HTML/stopwords). Tokenise using an appropriate tokeniser (word-level or subword). Apply TF-IDF vectorisation or tokenise for a transformer model. Split 80/20 train/test with stratification on the label.

Task 3: Text EDA — plot word frequency distributions per class using bar charts or word clouds. Visualise text length distributions. Plot the class label distribution. Identify the most discriminative words using TF-IDF scores.

Task 4: Model Training — train a TF-IDF + Logistic Regression baseline and a TF-IDF + Random Forest model. If time allows, fine-tune a lightweight transformer. Evaluate using a confusion matrix and one primary metric (Accuracy or F1 Score).
Task 5: Model Comparison and Domain Insights — Identify the top 3 most important features using the model's built-in feature importance or coefficients.
preprocessing_requirements: Lowercase all text data to ensure uniformity.
Remove punctuation and stopwords to reduce noise and improve readability.
Tokenize the text into meaningful units (e.g., words or subwords) for better understanding of context.
Handle class imbalance through techniques like oversampling or undersampling if necessary.
aiml_problems.expectedApproach: Use logistic regression and random forest for the initial baseline models due to their interpretability and effectiveness in handling categorical targets. Fine-tuning a BERT model can provide state-of-the-art performance but requires more computational resources.

The task is to develop a system capable of automatically identifying and classifying these entities within a given piece of text, which can have significant applications in information retrieval, knowledge extraction, and text understanding systems.

Task 3: Text EDA — plot word frequency distributions per class using bar charts or word clouds. Visualise text length distributions. Plot the class label distribution. Identify the most discriminative words using TF-IDF scores.

Task 5: Model Comparison and Domain Insights — use SHAP values to explain individual predictions. Identify the top drivers and explain their real-world meaning in the context of the problem domain.

aiml_problems.problemStatement: Predict the likelihood of a Pima Indian woman having diabetes based on her medical history and physical characteristics. This dataset includes various health indicators such as number of pregnancies, glucose levels, blood pressure, skin thickness, insulin levels, body mass index (BMI), pedigree function, and age. The goal is to develop a model that can accurately predict if a patient has diabetes, which will help healthcare providers make informed decisions about patient care.

Task 4: Model Training — Train a single baseline model (e.g., Logistic Regression or Decision Tree). Report accuracy on test set. Use class_weight='balanced' or SMOTE to handle class imbalance. Evaluate using a confusion matrix and one primary metric (Accuracy or F1-Score).

Task 5: Model Comparison and Domain Insights — Identify the top 3 most important features using the model's built-in feature importance or coefficients. Discuss how these features relate to the development of diabetes in the context of Pima Indian women.

aiml_problems.problemStatement: The task involves developing a system to automatically classify music clips into 10 distinct genres based on their audio content. This can be used in various applications such as music recommendation systems, content tagging services, and automated music analysis tools. Given that the dataset consists of 1000 audio clips (30 seconds each) across 10 genres (blues, classical, country, disco, hip-hop, jazz, metal, pop, reggae, rock), the goal is to build a robust model capable of accurately identifying these genres without manual intervention.
tasks: Task 1: Data Loading and Audio Inspection — load the dataset. Display the waveform and spectrogram of one sample per class. Report sampling rate (22050 Hz), clip duration distribution (30 seconds), and class balance (100 samples per class). Check for silent clips or clipping artefacts.

Task 2: Audio Feature Extraction — extract MFCC features (13–40 coefficients) from each clip. Optionally extract mel-spectrograms for deep learning approaches. Normalise features. Split 80/20 with stratification.
Task 3: Audio EDA — plot MFCC feature distributions per class. Visualise mel-spectrograms side by side for different classes. Plot clip duration and sampling rate distributions.

Task 4: Model Training — train a Random Forest on MFCC features as a baseline. Also train a simple MLP or 1D CNN on MFCC sequences. Evaluate using metrics appropriate for this target type. Plot a confusion matrix (classification) or residual plot (regression).

Task 5: Model Comparison and Domain Insights — use feature importance or permutation importance to identify the top 5 most predictive features. Explain what each implies in the real-world context of the problem.
preprocessing_requirements: Load the dataset containing 30-second audio clips and corresponding genre labels.
Extract MFCC features and optionally mel-spectrograms from the audio clips.
Ensure class balance during splitting; use stratified sampling to maintain equal representation of all genres in training and validation sets.
aiml_problems.expectedApproach: Random Forest and 1D Convolutional Neural Network (CNN). The Random Forest algorithm is suitable due to its ability to handle non-linear relationships and provide feature importance scores. A 1D CNN can capture temporal patterns effectively, making it a strong candidate for time-series data like audio recordings.

Task 5: Model Comparison and Domain Insights — use SHAP values to explain individual predictions. Identify the top drivers and explain their real-world meaning in the context of the problem domain, such as how certain features like color intensity or texture patterns influence the diagnosis.

tasks: Task 1: Data Loading and Exploration — load the dataset for 'Recommendation Systems'. Display shape, dtypes, first 10 rows, and per-column missing value counts. aiml_problems.problemStatement: Develop a climate prediction system to forecast daily temperature (in degrees Celsius) based on atmospheric conditions recorded at the Max Planck Institute in Jena, Germany. The dataset contains 14 meteorological variables measured every 10 minutes over a period from 2009 to 2016. This system will help researchers and environmental scientists understand and predict future climate trends more accurately, which can be crucial for planning and policy-making related to climate change mitigation and adaptation strategies.
tasks: Task 1: Data Loading and Time Series Inspection — load the dataset. Plot the full time series for each key variable such as 'T' (temperature), 'p' (pressure), 'rh' (relative humidity), 'w' (wind speed), 'wd' (wind direction), 'rain' (precipitation), etc. Report the time range, sampling frequency, and any obvious gaps or anomalies. Check for stationarity using the ADF test.

Task 2: Time Series Preprocessing — handle missing timestamps and interpolate gaps. Apply differencing or log-transform if the series is non-stationary. Create lag features and rolling window statistics as additional features. Use a time-ordered train/test split (no random shuffle).

Task 3: Time Series EDA — decompose the series into trend, seasonality, and residual. Plot autocorrelation (ACF) and partial autocorrelation (PACF). Visualise seasonal patterns using box plots grouped by month/hour/day. Plot rolling mean and standard deviation to confirm stationarity.

Task 4: Model Training — train an ARIMA baseline and a gradient-boosted model (LightGBM) using lag features. Evaluate using metrics appropriate for this target type. Plot a residual plot (not a confusion matrix since it’s not applicable for regression targets).

Task 5: Model Comparison and Domain Insights — Use feature importance or permutation importance to identify the top 5 most predictive features. Explain what each implies in the real-world context of the problem, such as how changes in wind direction ('wd') or relative humidity ('rh') affect temperature predictions.
preprocessing_requirements: Interpolate missing values for 'T', 'p', 'rh', 'w', 'wd', 'rain', etc., ensuring continuity in the time series.
Create lag features for 'T' by shifting the temperature data by one or multiple steps to capture temporal dependencies.
Check for stationarity using Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) test and apply transformations like differencing or log transformation if necessary.
aiml_problems.expectedApproach: For Task 4, use ARIMA as a strong baseline for time-series forecasting due to its ability to model trends and seasonality. For the gradient-boosted model, LightGBM is chosen because it excels in handling large datasets and can capture complex nonlinear relationships. Both models are suitable for regression tasks, making them ideal for predicting continuous values like temperature.

tasks: Task 1: Data Loading and Time Series Inspection — load the dataset. Plot the full time series for each key variable such as t (temperature), p (pressure), u (specific humidity), v (wind speed), etc. Report the time range, sampling frequency, which is every 10 minutes, and any obvious gaps or anomalies. Check for stationarity using the ADF test.

Task 2: Time Series Preprocessing — handle missing timestamps and interpolate gaps for features like t, p, u, v. Apply differencing or log-transform if the series is non-stationary for t. Create lag features and rolling window statistics for features such as t_lag_1, t_rolling_mean_7d, t_rolling_std_7d as additional features. Use a time-ordered train/test split (no random shuffle) for features including t, p, u, v.

Task 3: Time Series EDA — decompose the series for temperature (t) into trend, seasonality, and residual. Plot autocorrelation (ACF) and partial autocorrelation (PACF) specifically for temperature. Visualize seasonal patterns using box plots grouped by month, hour, day for temperature. Plot rolling mean and standard deviation to confirm stationarity for temperature.

Task 4: Model Training — train an ARIMA/SARIMA model as a statistical baseline for predicting temperature (t). Also train an LSTM or Transformer-based forecasting model for t. Compare using rolling-origin cross-validation. Evaluate with a full metric suite including MAE, RMSE, and R-squared for t. Plot learning curves to diagnose bias/variance for t. Use cross-validation (k=5) rather than a single train/test split for t, p, u, v.

Task 5: Model Comparison and Domain Insights — use SHAP values to explain individual predictions for temperature (t). Task 6: Model Deployment Readiness — export the best model using joblib or pickle for t. Write a predict() function that accepts raw input in the same format as the original features (e.g., t, p, u, v) and returns a prediction with a confidence score for t. Discuss what monitoring would be needed in production for t, p, u, v.

Both models should be evaluated on the same validation set to ensure fair comparison.

aiml_problems.problemStatement: Develop an anomaly detection system to identify potential cyber attacks on a network based on historical traffic data. This dataset contains detailed information about network connections, including their duration, type, size, and various flags that can indicate malicious activity. The goal is to classify each connection as either normal or one of four types of attacks: Denial of Service (DoS), Probing, Reverse to Local (R2L), and User to Root (U2R). The system will help cybersecurity professionals proactively monitor and respond to suspicious activities.
tasks: Task 1: Data Loading and Exploration — load the dataset for 'Anomaly Detection'. Display shape, dtypes, first 10 rows, and per-column missing value counts. Report descriptive statistics and identify any data quality issues specific to this domain such as missing values in 'duration', imbalanced classes, or unusual distributions in 'dst_host_count'.

Task 2: Data Preprocessing — handle missing values (impute or drop as appropriate). Encode categorical features like 'protocol_type' and 'flag' using LabelEncoder or OneHotEncoder as appropriate. Scale numerical features such as 'src_bytes' and 'dst_bytes' using StandardScaler or MinMaxScaler. Use stratified split for classification to ensure balanced representation of all classes in training and testing sets.

Task 3: Anomaly EDA — plot the class imbalance ratio using a pie chart or bar graph. Visualize the distribution of key numeric features such as 'num_root', 'num_access_files', and 'srv_count' split by normal vs anomalous samples using histograms or box plots. Plot a correlation heatmap and identify features most strongly correlated with the anomaly label, such as 'rerror_rate', 'serror_rate', and 'srv_rerror_rate'.

Task 4: Model Training — Train at least 2 models of different families (e.g., one linear like Logistic Regression and one tree-based like Random Forest). Compare performance using metrics like accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC-ROC for both models. Evaluate using metrics appropriate for this target type. Plot a confusion matrix for the Random Forest model and compare it with the Logistic Regression model’s confusion matrix. Justify which model performs better given the imbalanced nature of the dataset.

Task 5: Model Comparison and Domain Insights — Use feature importance or permutation importance to identify the top 5 most predictive features. For example, 'root_shell', 'same_srv_rate', 'diff_srv_rate', 'srv_serror_rate', and 'srv_rerror_rate'. Explain what each implies in the real-world context of the problem, such as how frequent access attempts ('root_shell') or high error rates during service requests ('srv_serror_rate') could indicate a potential attack.
preprocessing_requirements: Handling missing values in 'duration'
Encoding 'protocol_type' and 'flag' using LabelEncoder or OneHotEncoder
Scaling numerical features like 'src_bytes' and 'dst_bytes'
aiml_problems.expectedApproach: Logistic Regression and Random Forest classifiers. Logistic Regression is chosen due to its interpretability and suitability for binary classification problems. Random Forest is selected for its ability to handle non-linear relationships and provide feature importance scores, making it suitable for identifying critical features in complex datasets.

aiml_problems.problemStatement: Develop an effective credit card fraud detection system to prevent unauthorized transactions and reduce financial losses. Given the highly imbalanced nature of the dataset, where only 0.172% of transactions are fraudulent, the challenge is to build a robust model capable of identifying these rare cases while maintaining high overall accuracy and minimizing false negatives.

Use k-fold cross-validation (k=5) rather than a single train/test split.

Task 5: Model Comparison and Domain Insights — Use SHAP values to explain individual predictions. Identify the top drivers and explain their real-world meaning in the context of the problem domain, focusing on how factors like transaction amount or time since first transaction influence fraud likelihood.

Task 6: Model Deployment Readiness — export the best model using joblib or pickle. - **Logistic Regression**: Useful as a baseline model to compare against more complex models and for interpretability purposes, especially when dealing with a highly imbalanced dataset.

aiml_problems.problemStatement: Given a synthetic binary classification dataset derived from financial customer behavior, develop a model to predict fraudulent transactions based on historical customer data. This dataset includes 20 numerical features representing various aspects of customer behavior such as transaction amount, frequency, time of day, and other potential fraud signals, along with a binary label indicating whether a transaction was fraudulent or not.
tasks: Task 1: Data Loading and Exploration — load the dataset for 'Feature Engineering'. Display shape, dtypes, first 10 rows, and per-column missing value counts. Report descriptive statistics and identify any data quality issues specific to this domain, such as outliers in transaction amounts or inconsistencies in date formats.

Task 2: Data Preprocessing — handle missing values (impute or drop as appropriate). Encode categorical features using LabelEncoder or OneHotEncoder as appropriate. Scale numerical features using StandardScaler or MinMaxScaler. Use stratified split for classification. Address class imbalance using SMOTE or class_weight='balanced'. Ensure that all preprocessing steps are justified in the context of financial fraud detection.

Task 3: Exploratory Data Analysis — Visualise the target distribution. Plot the top-10 feature correlations with the target. Create 2–3 domain-specific plots relevant to 'Feature Engineering', such as a heatmap of correlation between features, box plots comparing fraudulent vs non-fraudulent transactions, and scatter plots showing the relationship between transaction amount and time of day.

Task 4: Model Training — Train at least 2 models of different families (e.g., one linear, one tree-based). Compare performance and justify which is more appropriate for this problem. Use class_weight='balanced' or SMOTE to handle class imbalance. Evaluate using metrics appropriate for this target type, such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC. Plot a confusion matrix to visualize prediction errors.

Task 5: Model Comparison and Domain Insights — Use feature importance or permutation importance to identify the top 5 most predictive features. Explain what each implies in the real-world context of the problem, such as how transaction frequency might correlate with higher risk of fraud.
preprocessing_requirements: Impute missing values in 'transaction_amount'
Encode 'transaction_time_of_day' using OneHotEncoder
Scale numerical features including 'transaction_amount', 'frequency_of_transactions', and 'time_since_last_transaction'
aiml_problems.expectedApproach: For this dataset, we can use Logistic Regression and Random Forest as two different types of models. Logistic Regression is chosen because it works well with binary classification problems and can provide interpretable coefficients. Random Forest is selected due to its ability to handle non-linear relationships and interactions among features without explicit feature engineering, making it robust against overfitting.

aiml_problems.problemStatement: The goal is to develop a machine learning model to detect Higgs bosons in high-energy physics experiments. Given the vast amount of data generated from simulated particle collisions, distinguishing between Higgs boson signals and background processes is crucial for advancing our understanding of fundamental particle interactions. This dataset contains 28 numerical kinematic features derived from these simulations, which need to be carefully engineered to improve detection accuracy.
tasks: Task 1: Data Loading and Exploration — load the dataset for 'Feature Engineering'. Display shape, dtypes, first 10 rows, and per-column missing value counts. Report descriptive statistics and identify any data quality issues specific to this domain, such as outliers or non-normal distributions in kinematic features.

Task 2: Data Preprocessing — handle missing values (impute or drop as appropriate). Encode categorical features using LabelEncoder or OneHotEncoder as appropriate. Scale numerical features using StandardScaler or MinMaxScaler. Use stratified split for classification. Address class imbalance using SMOTE or class_weight='balanced'. Ensure all steps are clearly documented and justified based on the domain knowledge.

Task 3: Exploratory Data Analysis — Visualise the target distribution. Plot the top-10 feature correlations with the target. Create 2–3 domain-specific plots relevant to 'Feature Engineering', such as scatter plots of feature pairs showing potential relationships or histograms of key features.

Task 4: Model Training — Build a full ML pipeline using sklearn Pipeline or equivalent. Train at least 3 models including one ensemble (e.g., XGBoost or LightGBM) and one interpretable baseline (e.g., Logistic Regression). Perform hyperparameter tuning using GridSearchCV or Optuna. Use class_weight='balanced' or SMOTE to handle class imbalance. Evaluate with a full metric suite including precision, recall, F1-score, ROC-AUC, and confusion matrix. Plot learning curves to diagnose bias/variance. Use k-fold cross-validation (k=5) rather than a single train/test split.

Task 5: Model Comparison and Domain Insights — Use SHAP values to explain individual predictions. Identify the top drivers and explain their real-world meaning in the context of the problem domain, such as how certain kinematic features contribute to the detection of Higgs bosons.

Task 6: Model Deployment Readiness — export the best model using joblib or pickle. Write a predict() function that accepts raw input in the same format as the original features and returns a prediction with confidence score. Discuss what monitoring would be needed in production, considering the high stakes of false positives and false negatives in particle physics research.
preprocessing_requirements: Imputation or dropping of missing values for features like 'feature1', 'feature2', etc.
Encoding of categorical features using LabelEncoder or OneHotEncoder for features like 'category_feature'
Scaling of numerical features using StandardScaler or MinMaxScaler for features like 'feature3', 'feature4'
aiml_problems.expectedApproach: 2-3 specific ML algorithms suited for this exact dataset include XGBoost, LightGBM, and Logistic Regression. XGBoost and LightGBM are chosen due to their performance in handling large datasets and dealing with imbalanced classes, while Logistic Regression serves as an interpretable baseline.

The goal here is to use dimensionality reduction techniques to improve the performance of a classification model on this dataset.

Task 2: Data Preprocessing — handle missing values (impute or drop as appropriate). Encode categorical features using LabelEncoder or OneHotEncoder as appropriate. Scale numerical features using StandardScaler or MinMaxScaler. Use stratified split for classification.
Task 3: Exploratory Data Analysis — Visualise the target distribution. Plot the top-10 feature correlations with the target. Create 2–3 domain-specific plots relevant to 'Dimensionality Reduction'.

Task 4: Model Training — Train a single baseline model (e.g., Logistic Regression or Decision Tree) on the preprocessed data. Report accuracy on the test set. Evaluate using a confusion matrix and one primary metric (Accuracy or F1 score).

aiml_problems.problemStatement: The task involves recognizing handwritten digits from a dataset of 8x8 pixel grayscale images. This dataset can be used to develop a model that accurately classifies these images into their corresponding digits (0 through 9). Given the nature of the dataset, we will focus on dimensionality reduction techniques before applying them to a classification model to improve accuracy and reduce computational complexity.
tasks: Task 1: Data Loading and Exploration — load the dataset for 'Dimensionality Reduction'. Display shape, dtypes, first 10 rows, and per-column missing value counts. Report descriptive statistics and identify any data quality issues specific to this domain, such as missing values or outliers in pixel intensities.

Task 2: Data Preprocessing — handle missing values (impute or drop as appropriate). Encode categorical features using LabelEncoder or OneHotEncoder as appropriate. Scale numerical features using StandardScaler or MinMaxScaler. Use stratified split for classification.

Task 3: Exploratory Data Analysis — Visualise the target distribution. Plot the top-10 feature correlations with the target. Create 2–3 domain-specific plots relevant to 'Dimensionality Reduction', such as scatter plots of pixel intensities against digit classes or heatmaps of feature importance after dimensionality reduction.

Task 4: Model Training — Train at least 2 models of different families (e.g., one linear, one tree-based). For example, use Logistic Regression and Random Forest Classifier. Compare performance and justify which is more appropriate for this problem. Evaluate using metrics appropriate for this target type, such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC. Plot a confusion matrix for both models.

Task 5: Model Comparison and Domain Insights — Use feature importance or permutation importance to identify the top 5 most predictive features. Explain what each implies in the real-world context of the problem, such as how certain pixel areas contribute to the recognition of specific digits.
preprocessing_requirements: Impute missing values if present in the pixel intensity features using SimpleImputer.
Encode the target variable using LabelEncoder since it is already in integer form.
Scale all numerical features including pixel intensity features using StandardScaler to normalize the range.
Ensure the dataset is balanced using StratifiedShuffleSplit for training and testing splits.
aiml_problems.expectedApproach: Use PCA (Principal Component Analysis) for dimensionality reduction followed by either Logistic Regression or Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) for classification. PCA helps reduce the number of dimensions while retaining important information, making subsequent classification easier and faster. LDA can further enhance the separation between classes based on the reduced features.

aiml_problems.problemStatement: The task involves recognizing individuals based on facial images collected from 40 subjects under various conditions such as lighting, facial expressions, and accessories. This dataset provides a rich ground for exploring advanced techniques in computer vision and machine learning, specifically focusing on dimensionality reduction and classification tasks.
tasks: Task 1: Data Loading and Inspection — load the dataset. Display sample images from each class (at least 5 per class). Report the total number of images, image dimensions, colour channels, and class distribution. Check for any corrupted or unusually sized images.

Task 2: Image Preprocessing and Augmentation — resize all images to 64x64 pixels and normalise pixel values between 0 and 1. Apply data augmentation (random flip, rotation, zoom) to the training set only. Split the dataset into training and testing sets with a ratio of 80/20, ensuring stratified sampling to maintain balanced classes. Report class balance after splitting.

Task 3: Visual EDA — display a grid of sample images from each class. Plot the class distribution as a bar chart. For tabular metadata (if available), plot distributions of key attributes. Visualise mean and variance images per class.

Task 4: Model Training — fine-tune a pretrained model (e.g., ResNet-50 or EfficientNet-B0) using transfer learning. Freeze the base layers and train only the classification head first, then unfreeze and fine-tune end-to-end. Evaluate with a full metric suite including accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC. Perform k-fold cross-validation (k=5) instead of a single train/test split to ensure robustness. Plot learning curves to diagnose bias/variance.

Task 5: Model Comparison and Domain Insights — use SHAP values to explain individual predictions. Identify the top drivers and explain their real-world meaning in the context of the problem domain. Discuss how these insights can improve future models or understanding of human faces.

Task 6: Model Deployment Readiness — export the best model using joblib or pickle. Write a predict() function that accepts raw input in the same format as the original features (4096-dimensional vector) and returns a prediction with a confidence score. Discuss what monitoring would be needed in production to ensure the model remains accurate over time.
preprocessing_requirements: Resize images to 64x64 pixels
Normalise pixel values between 0 and 1
Apply data augmentation (random flip, rotation, zoom) to the training set only
Ensure stratified sampling during splitting to maintain balanced classes
expectedApproach: ResNet-50 with transfer learning for its pre-trained weights and deep architecture suitable for complex image classification tasks.
EfficientNet-B0 for its efficient design and better performance compared to other architectures given the limited dataset size.

aiml_problems.problemStatement: Given the MNIST handwritten digit dataset represented as flat 784-column tabular data, your task is to apply Principal Component Analysis (PCA) for dimensionality reduction before classifying the digits into their respective classes. This dataset contains 70,000 images of handwritten digits from 0 to 9, each image being a 28x28 pixel grid, resulting in 784 features ranging from 0 to 255. Your goal is to accurately classify these digits while understanding how PCA affects the model performance.
tasks: Task 1: Data Loading and Exploration — load the dataset for 'PCA'. Display shape, dtypes, first 10 rows, and per-column missing value counts. Report descriptive statistics and identify any data quality issues specific to this domain. Note that there are no missing values in this dataset, but ensure all 784 features are correctly loaded.

Task 2: Data Preprocessing — handle missing values (impute or drop as appropriate). Encode categorical features using LabelEncoder or OneHotEncoder as appropriate. Scale numerical features using StandardScaler or MinMaxScaler. Use stratified split for classification. Since there are no missing values, focus on scaling and encoding the target variable.

Task 3: Exploratory Data Analysis — Visualise the target distribution. Plot the top-10 feature correlations with the target. Create 2–3 domain-specific plots relevant to 'PCA', such as scatter plots of the first two principal components or histograms of the transformed features.

Task 4: Model Training — Train at least 2 models of different families (e.g., one linear, one tree-based). Compare performance and justify which is more appropriate for this problem. Evaluate using metrics appropriate for this target type. Plot a confusion matrix (classification) or residual plot (regression). For example, train a Logistic Regression and a Decision Tree Classifier, compare their accuracy and F1 scores, and discuss which performs better given the class imbalance.

Task 5: Model Comparison and Domain Insights — Use feature importance or permutation importance to identify the top 5 most predictive features. Explain what each implies in the real-world context of the problem. For instance, discuss how certain pixel regions contribute to the classification of digits.
preprocessing_requirements: Scale numerical features using StandardScaler or MinMaxScaler since they range from 0 to 255.
Encode the target variable using LabelEncoder or OneHotEncoder if you choose to use OneHotEncoder for model training.
Ensure the dataset is split into training and testing sets using a stratified approach to maintain class balance.
aiml_problems.expectedApproach: Two specific ML algorithms suited for this exact dataset include Logistic Regression and Random Forest Classifier. Logistic Regression is chosen because it can provide interpretable coefficients, while Random Forest provides robustness through ensemble methods and handles non-linear relationships well.

aiml_problems.problemStatement: The task is to predict the research topic of scientific papers based on their citation patterns and text content within the Cora Citation Network Graph Dataset. This dataset contains information about 2708 research papers, each represented as a node in a graph with 1433-dimensional bag-of-words (BoW) features and connected via citation links. Each paper falls into one of 7 categories representing different research topics.
tasks: Task 1: Data Loading and Graph Inspection — load the graph dataset. Report the number of nodes (2708 papers), edges (5429 citations), and node features (1433 BoW features). Visualize a subgraph of 50 nodes. Check degree distribution and identify hub nodes (papers that have been cited frequently).

Task 2: Data Preprocessing — handle missing values (impute or drop as appropriate). Encode categorical features (research topics) using LabelEncoder or OneHotEncoder as appropriate. Scale numerical features (BoW features) using StandardScaler or MinMaxScaler. Use stratified split for classification to ensure balanced training and testing sets.

Task 3: Exploratory Data Analysis — Visualize the target distribution (research topics). Plot the top-10 feature correlations with the target (research topics). Create 2–3 domain-specific plots relevant to 't-SNE' such as visualizing the BoW feature space or citation network topology.

Task 4: Model Training — Train at least 2 models of different families (e.g., one linear like Logistic Regression, one tree-based like Random Forest). Compare performance and justify which is more appropriate for this problem. Evaluate using metrics appropriate for this target type (classification accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score). Plot a confusion matrix to visualize prediction errors.

Task 5: Model Comparison and Domain Insights — Use feature importance or permutation importance to identify the top 5 most predictive features. Explain what each implies in the real-world context of the problem, such as identifying key words or themes that influence the research topic.
preprocessing_requirements: Impute missing BoW features if present using mean imputation or drop rows with missing values.
Encode the 7 research topics using OneHotEncoder since they are nominal categorical variables.
Scale the 1433 BoW features to ensure all features contribute equally to the model predictions.
aiml_problems.expectedApproach: Logistic Regression and Random Forest classifiers. Logistic Regression is chosen due to its interpretability and suitability for binary or multi-class problems. Random Forest is selected for its robustness and ability to handle high-dimensional data without overfitting.

aiml_problems.problemStatement: The HIGGS Boson Detection dataset originates from simulated particle collision experiments aiming to differentiate between Higgs boson signals and background noise. The goal is to develop a robust machine learning system capable of accurately identifying Higgs bosons among millions of potential events. This is crucial for advancing our understanding of fundamental particles and interactions in high-energy physics.
tasks: Task 1: Data Loading and Exploration — load the dataset for 't-SNE'. Display shape, dtypes, first 10 rows, and per-column missing value counts. Report descriptive statistics and identify any data quality issues specific to this domain, such as outliers or redundant features related to particle kinematics.

Task 2: Data Preprocessing — handle missing values (impute or drop as appropriate). Encode categorical features using LabelEncoder or OneHotEncoder as appropriate. Scale numerical features using StandardScaler or MinMaxScaler. Use stratified split for classification. Address class imbalance using SMOTE or class_weight='balanced'. Ensure all preprocessing steps align with the physical constraints of the dataset, such as maintaining units consistent across features.

Task 3: Exploratory Data Analysis — Visualise the target distribution. Plot the top-10 feature correlations with the target. Create 2–3 domain-specific plots relevant to 't-SNE', such as scatter plots of key kinematic features against the target or histograms of feature distributions segmented by the target class.

Task 4: Model Training — Build a full ML pipeline using sklearn Pipeline or equivalent. Train at least 3 models including one ensemble (e.g., XGBoost or LightGBM) and one interpretable baseline (e.g., Logistic Regression). Perform hyperparameter tuning using GridSearchCV or Optuna. Use class_weight='balanced' or SMOTE to handle class imbalance. Evaluate with full metric suite including precision, recall, F1-score, ROC-AUC, and confusion matrix. Plot learning curves to diagnose bias/variance. Use k-fold cross-validation (k=5) rather than a single train/test split to ensure robust model generalization.

Task 5: Model Comparison and Domain Insights — Use SHAP values to explain individual predictions. Identify the top drivers and explain their real-world meaning in the context of the problem domain, such as how momentum or energy transfer impacts the likelihood of a Higgs boson detection.

Task 6: Model Deployment Readiness — export the best model using joblib or pickle. Write a predict() function that accepts raw input in the same format as the original features and returns a prediction with confidence score. Discuss what monitoring would be needed in production, considering the need for continuous validation of model performance given the dynamic nature of experimental data.
preprocessing_requirements: Imputation of missing values in features like mass, momentum, and angle using mean imputation or median imputation depending on the distribution of the data.
Encoding of categorical features if present, ensuring they do not introduce non-linearity or redundancy into the model.
Scaling of numerical features to maintain consistency in units and scale, especially when dealing with large variations in magnitudes.
Handling class imbalance through techniques like SMOTE or setting class weights to balance the training process effectively.
aiml_problems.expectedApproach: For this dataset, consider using XGBoost and LightGBM due to their efficiency and ability to handle large datasets while providing good performance. Additionally, logistic regression can serve as a baseline to compare interpretability and performance. These models are suitable because they can capture complex relationships in the kinematic features and provide a balanced approach between accuracy and computational efficiency.

aiml_problems.problemStatement: The goal is to recognize handwritten digits using autoencoders. Given a dataset of 8x8 pixel grayscale images of handwritten digits from 0 to 9, the task is to train an autoencoder to compress these images into a lower-dimensional representation and then reconstruct them back to their original form. This can be used for various applications such as image compression, denoising, and generating new handwritten digits.
tasks: Task 1: Data Loading and Exploration — load the dataset for 'Handwritten Digits Recognition'. Display shape, dtypes, first 10 rows, and per-column missing value counts. Report descriptive statistics and identify any data quality issues specific to this domain.

Task 2: Data Preprocessing — handle missing values (impute or drop as appropriate). Encode categorical features using LabelEncoder or OneHotEncoder as appropriate. Scale numerical features using StandardScaler or MinMaxScaler. Use stratified split for classification.

Task 3: Exploratory Data Analysis — Visualise the target distribution. Plot the top-10 feature correlations with the target. Create 2–3 domain-specific plots relevant to 'Handwritten Digits Recognition'.

Task 4: Generative Model Training — implement the generative architecture appropriate for 'Handwritten Digits Recognition'. Train on the dataset and generate samples. Evaluate using appropriate metrics (FID for images, BLEU/ROUGE for text, perceptual loss as applicable).
Task 5: Model Comparison and Domain Insights — Identify the top 3 most important features using the model's built-in feature importance or coefficients.
preprocessing_requirements: Handle missing values if present in the dataset. Since the dataset does not have missing values, this step might involve checking for anomalies or outliers.
Encode the target variable 'digit' using LabelEncoder since it is a multiclass classification problem.
Scale all numerical features between 0 and 1 using MinMaxScaler to ensure that the input to the autoencoder is normalized.
aiml_problems.expectedApproach: For this dataset, we can use a simple Variational Autoencoder (VAE) or a Denoising Autoencoder (DAE). VAEs are good for generating new samples while maintaining a probabilistic latent space which can help in understanding the underlying distribution of the data. DAEs are useful for noise removal and preserving the integrity of the original data during reconstruction. Both models are suitable for this task given the nature of the data and the requirement for image generation and compression.

aiml_problems.problemStatement: Develop an automated system to accurately classify handwritten digits from the MNIST dataset, which consists of 28x28 grayscale images of digits 0 through 9. This system will be used in various applications such as optical character recognition in bank checks and digital document processing.
tasks: Task 1: Data Loading and Inspection — load the dataset. Display sample images from each class (at least 5 per class). Report the total number of images, image dimensions (28x28 pixels), colour channels (grayscale), and class distribution. Check for any corrupted or unusually sized images.

Task 2: Image Preprocessing and Augmentation — resize all images and normalise pixel values. Apply data augmentation (random flip, rotation, zoom) to the training set only. Split 80/20 with stratification. Report class balance after split.

Task 3: Visual EDA — display a grid of sample images from each class. Plot the class distribution as a bar chart. For tabular metadata (if available), plot distributions of key attributes. Visualise mean and variance images per class.

Task 4: Model Training — train a baseline CNN and a pretrained model (e.g., MobileNetV2) with transfer learning. Evaluate using metrics appropriate for this target type (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score). Plot a confusion matrix (classification) or residual plot (regression).

Task 5: Model Comparison and Domain Insights — use feature importance or permutation importance to identify the top 5 most predictive features. Explain what each implies in the real-world context of the problem (e.g., how certain pixel patterns contribute to recognizing specific digits).
preprocessing_requirements: Resize all images to 28x28 pixels
Normalise pixel values between 0 and 1
Apply random flips, rotations, and zooms to the training set only
aiml_problems.expectedApproach: For this dataset, two suitable approaches could be: 1) Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) due to its effectiveness in handling image data; 2) Transfer Learning using MobileNetV2, leveraging pre-trained weights on large datasets like ImageNet to improve performance. Both methods leverage the spatial hierarchies found in images and can handle the high-dimensional input space efficiently.

aiml_problems.problemStatement: The HIGGS Boson Detection dataset aims to classify whether a given set of particle collision events contains a Higgs boson signal or not. This task involves distinguishing high-energy physics phenomena through complex kinematic features measured during particle interactions. The goal is to develop a robust autoencoder-based system capable of both accurately classifying these events and generating new, realistic event patterns that can help physicists better understand underlying physical processes.
tasks: Task 1: Data Loading and Exploration — load the dataset for 'HIGGS Boson Detection'. Display shape, dtypes, first 10 rows, and per-column missing value counts. Report descriptive statistics and identify any data quality issues specific to this domain such as outliers or skewed distributions in the kinematic features.

Task 2: Data Preprocessing — handle missing values (impute or drop as appropriate) using median imputation for numerical features due to their nature. Encode categorical features if present (though none are listed here). Scale numerical features using StandardScaler to normalize them across all features. Use stratified split for classification into training and testing sets while ensuring similar proportions of signal and background classes. Address class imbalance using SMOTE or setting class_weight='balanced' in the classifier.

Task 3: Exploratory Data Analysis — Visualise the target distribution using a histogram. Plot the top-10 feature correlations with the target using a correlation matrix heatmap. Create a scatter plot of two representative kinematic features against the target to show how they vary with signal and background classifications.

Task 4: Generative Model Training — implement a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) suitable for 'HIGGS Boson Detection'. Train on the dataset and generate samples to simulate new particle collision events. Evaluate using Inception Score (IS) or Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) to measure the realism of generated samples compared to real ones.

Task 5: Model Comparison and Domain Insights — use SHAP values to explain individual predictions made by the VAE. Identify the top drivers and explain their real-world meaning in terms of key kinematic features like transverse momentum or particle decay paths. Discuss how these insights could inform further experimental design in particle physics.

Task 6: Model Deployment Readiness — export the best VAE model using joblib or pickle. Write a predict() function that accepts raw input in the same format as the original features and returns a probability of being a signal along with its confidence score. Discuss what continuous monitoring would be needed in a production environment, including regular retraining and validation against updated datasets.
preprocessing_requirements: Impute missing values using median imputation for numerical features
Scale numerical features using StandardScaler
Use stratified split for classification to ensure balanced classes
Address class imbalance using SMOTE or class_weight='balanced'
expectedApproach: Variational Autoencoder (VAE) - Since the target is binary and the dataset involves complex distributions typical in high-energy physics, a VAE is well-suited for generating realistic samples and understanding latent spaces.
Random Forest Classifier - For initial baseline performance, a Random Forest can provide strong out-of-the-box results and interpretability of feature importance.
Gradient Boosting Classifier - Another strong option for handling non-linear relationships and interaction effects among features.

This dataset is widely used for face attribute prediction and GAN training. In this assessment, you will work on a multi-label classification task where the goal is to predict these attributes from face images. The real-world application could be in developing a system that can accurately recognize and classify various facial attributes, which has applications in social media analytics, advertising, and user interface design.
tasks: Task 1: Data Loading and Inspection — load the dataset. Display sample images from each class (at least 5 per class) based on the 'Smiling' attribute. Task 2: Image Preprocessing and Augmentation — resize all images to 64x64 pixels and normalise pixel values between 0 and 1. Apply data augmentation techniques like random horizontal flips, rotations, and zooms to the training set only. Split the dataset into training and validation sets in an 80/20 ratio with stratified sampling to ensure balanced classes. Report class balance after splitting.

Task 3: Visual EDA — display a grid of sample images from each class ('Smiling' and 'Not Smiling') along with their corresponding labels. Plot the class distribution of the 'Smiling' attribute as a bar chart. For the tabular metadata, if available, plot the distributions of key attributes like 'Young' and 'Eyeglasses'. Task 4: Model Training — train a baseline CNN and a pretrained model (e.g., MobileNetV2) with transfer learning. Evaluate using metrics appropriate for this target type such as accuracy, F1 score, precision, recall, and ROC AUC. Plot a confusion matrix to understand misclassifications.

Task 5: Model Comparison and Domain Insights — use feature importance or permutation importance to identify the top 5 most predictive features related to the 'Smiling' attribute. Additionally, leveraging a pretrained model like MobileNetV2 with transfer learning can provide strong performance due to its pre-trained weights on large datasets. Transfer learning helps in reducing overfitting and speeds up the training process by utilizing existing knowledge.

aiml_problems.problemStatement: The goal of this project is to develop a robust system capable of recognizing fine-grained objects within the CIFAR-100 dataset, which consists of 32x32 RGB images across 100 categories divided into 20 superclasses. This task requires advanced computer vision techniques to distinguish between closely related classes such as different breeds of dogs, types of cars, and various fruits, which present significant challenges due to their high similarity and subtle differences. The solution will contribute to applications like autonomous vehicle navigation, environmental monitoring, and consumer electronics design where accurate object identification is crucial.

aiml_problems.problemStatement: Forecasting electricity market prices in Australia’s New South Wales (NSW) can help energy providers make informed decisions on supply and demand management. This dataset captures hourly changes in electricity demand and price, enabling us to predict whether the NSW electricity price will rise or fall based on various factors such as demand patterns, transfers between markets, and other economic indicators. The goal is to build a model that can accurately predict the direction of the electricity price, aiding in strategic planning and operational efficiency within the energy sector.

aiml_problems.problemStatement: Develop a reinforcement learning system to optimize the detection of Higgs bosons in high-energy physics experiments. The goal is to accurately classify events as either Higgs boson signals or background processes based on 28 kinematic features extracted from particle collision data. This task is crucial for improving the efficiency and accuracy of particle collider analysis, which can lead to significant advancements in our understanding of fundamental particles and forces in nature.
tasks: Task 1: Data Loading and Exploration — load the dataset for 'Reinforcement Learning'. Display shape, dtypes, first 10 rows, and per-column missing value counts. Report descriptive statistics and identify any data quality issues specific to this domain.

Task 2: Data Preprocessing — handle missing values (impute or drop as appropriate). Encode categorical features using LabelEncoder or OneHotEncoder as appropriate. Scale numerical features using StandardScaler or MinMaxScaler. Use stratified split for classification. Address class imbalance using SMOTE or class_weight='balanced'.

Task 3: Exploratory Data Analysis — Visualise the target distribution. Plot the top-10 feature correlations with the target. Create 2–3 domain-specific plots relevant to 'Reinforcement Learning', such as scatter plots of selected features against the target variable or histograms of feature distributions.

Task 4: Model Training — Build a full ML pipeline using sklearn Pipeline or equivalent. Train at least 3 models including one ensemble (e.g., XGBoost or LightGBM) and one interpretable baseline (e.g., Logistic Regression). Perform hyperparameter tuning using GridSearchCV or Optuna. Use class_weight='balanced' or SMOTE to handle class imbalance. Evaluate with full metric suite. Plot learning curves to diagnose bias/variance. Use k=5 cross-validation rather than a single train/test split.

Task 5: Model Comparison and Domain Insights — Use SHAP values to explain individual predictions. Identify the top drivers and explain their real-world meaning in the context of the problem domain. For example, interpret the impact of invariant mass, transverse momentum, or other key features on the final classification outcome.

Task 6: Model Deployment Readiness — export the best model using joblib or pickle. Write a predict() function that accepts raw input in the same format as the original features and returns a prediction with confidence score. Discuss what monitoring would be needed in production, such as regular retraining, performance degradation checks, and feature drift analysis.
preprocessing_requirements: Impute missing values in columns like 'pt1', 'eta1', 'phi1', etc., if present, or drop them depending on the impact on the analysis.
Encode categorical features like 'type_of_collision' using LabelEncoder or OneHotEncoder.
Scale numerical features like 'mass', 'energy', 'momentum', etc., using StandardScaler or MinMaxScaler.
Address class imbalance using SMOTE or set class_weight='balanced' during model training.
aiml_problems.expectedApproach: For Task 4, use XGBoost for its robustness and scalability, and Logistic Regression as a simple yet effective baseline. Both models should be suitable for this classification task given the binary target and large number of features.

tasks: Task 1: Data Loading and Exploration — load the dataset for 'Q-Learning'. Display shape, dtypes, first 10 rows, and per-column missing value counts. Report descriptive statistics and identify any data quality issues specific to this domain, such as outliers in price or demand that might indicate errors in recording.

Task 2: Data Preprocessing — handle missing values (impute or drop as appropriate). Encode categorical features using LabelEncoder or OneHotEncoder as appropriate. Scale numerical features using StandardScaler or MinMaxScaler. Use stratified split for classification. Address class imbalance using SMOTE or class_weight='balanced'. For example, if 'nswprice' has significant class imbalance, use SMOTE to balance it out before training the models.

Task 3: Exploratory Data Analysis — Visualise the target distribution of 'nswprice'. Plot the top-10 feature correlations with 'nswprice'. aiml_problems.problemStatement: Given the MIT-BIH arrhythmia ECG heartbeat dataset, develop a Q-learning algorithm to classify heartbeats into five distinct arrhythmia types based on the 187 time-step ECG signal values per heartbeat. This task aims to automate the diagnosis of cardiac conditions, improving patient care through accurate and timely identification of arrhythmias.
tasks: Task 1: Data Loading and Time Series Inspection — load the dataset. Plot the full time series for each key variable such as 'time_step_1' to 'time_step_187'. Report the time range, sampling frequency, and any obvious gaps or anomalies. Check for stationarity using the ADF test.

Task 2: Time Series Preprocessing — handle missing timestamps and interpolate gaps. Apply differencing or log-transform if the series is non-stationary. Create lag features and rolling window statistics as additional features. Use a time-ordered train/test split (no random shuffle).

Task 3: Time Series EDA — decompose the series into trend, seasonality, and residual. Plot autocorrelation (ACF) and partial autocorrelation (PACF). Visualise seasonal patterns using box plots grouped by hour/day. Plot rolling mean and standard deviation to confirm stationarity.

Task 4: Model Training — train an ARIMA/SARIMA model as a statistical baseline. Also train an LSTM or Transformer-based forecasting model. Compare using rolling-origin cross-validation. Evaluate with full metric suite. Plot learning curves to diagnose bias/variance. Use cross-validation (k=5) rather than a single train/test split.

Task 5: Model Comparison and Domain Insights — use SHAP values to explain individual predictions. Identify the top drivers and explain their real-world meaning in the context of the problem domain. For instance, 'time_step_100' might indicate a critical point in the ECG signal affecting arrhythmia classification.

Task 6: Model Deployment Readiness — export the best model using joblib or pickle. Write a predict() function that accepts raw input in the same format as the original features ('time_step_1' to 'time_step_187') and returns a prediction with confidence score. Discuss what monitoring would be needed in production.
preprocessing_requirements: Handle missing values in 'time_step_1' to 'time_step_187' by interpolation.
Create lag features like 'lag_time_step_1', 'lag_time_step_2', etc., for each time step.
Check for class imbalance in the target labels and apply SMOTE if necessary.
aiml_problems.expectedApproach: For this dataset, we can use an LSTM network due to its ability to capture temporal dependencies in time-series data. Additionally, a Random Forest classifier could be used as it handles non-linear relationships well and provides interpretable results via feature importance scores.

aiml_problems.problemStatement: The goal is to develop a system that can automatically classify incoming SMS messages into two categories: ham (legitimate) and spam. The SMS Spam Collection Dataset contains 5574 SMS messages labeled as either spam or ham, making it ideal for training such a system. Given the class imbalance, it is crucial to ensure the model performs well even when dealing with the minority class (spam messages).

This task simulates a common application in Information Retrieval and Natural Language Processing where understanding and classifying text data is crucial.
tasks: Task 1: Data Loading and Text Inspection — load the dataset. Display 5 sample texts per class/label. Report vocabulary size, average token length, class distribution, and language distribution if multilingual. Identify any HTML artefacts, special characters, or encoding issues.

Task 2: Text Preprocessing — clean text (lowercase, remove punctuation/HTML/stopwords). Tokenise using an appropriate tokeniser (word-level or subword). Apply TF-IDF vectorisation or tokenise for a transformer model. Split 80/20 train/test with stratification on the label.

Task 3: Text EDA — plot word frequency distributions per class using bar charts or word clouds. Visualise text length distributions. Plot the class label distribution. Identify the most discriminative words using TF-IDF scores.

Task 4: Model Training — train a TF-IDF + Logistic Regression baseline and a TF-IDF + Random Forest model. If time allows, fine-tune a lightweight transformer. Evaluate using metrics appropriate for this target type. Plot a confusion matrix (classification) or residual plot (regression).

preprocessing_requirements: Lowercasing all text to standardize the format for further processing.

aiml_problems.expectedApproach: For this dataset, two suitable models are Logistic Regression and Random Forest due to their interpretability and effectiveness in handling categorical targets. Logistic Regression works well with linearly separable data and provides clear coefficients which can be interpreted as feature importances. Random Forest, being an ensemble method, can handle non-linear relationships and provide feature importances through the decision trees it builds. Fine-tuning a lightweight transformer like BERT could also improve performance but would require more computational resources.

aiml_problems.problemStatement: The RAVDESS Speech Emotion Recognition dataset contains 1440 high-quality audio recordings of 24 professional actors expressing eight different emotions. This dataset serves as a benchmark for Speech Emotion Recognition (SER) systems, which can have significant applications in human-computer interaction, mental health monitoring, and customer service technologies. The goal is to develop an accurate SER system that can classify spoken words into one of eight emotional categories based on their acoustic characteristics.
tasks: Task 1: Data Loading and Audio Inspection — load the dataset. Display the waveform and spectrogram of one sample per class. Report sampling rate, clip duration distribution, and class balance. Check for silent clips or clipping artefacts.

Task 2: Audio Feature Extraction — extract MFCC features (13–40 coefficients) from each clip. Optionally extract mel-spectrograms for deep learning approaches. Normalise features. Split 80/20 with stratification.
Task 3: Audio EDA — plot MFCC feature distributions per class. Visualise mel-spectrograms side by side for different classes. Plot clip duration and sampling rate distributions.

Task 4: Model Training — train a Random Forest on MFCC features as a baseline. Also train a simple MLP or 1D CNN on MFCC sequences. Evaluate using metrics appropriate for this target type. Plot a confusion matrix (classification) or residual plot (regression).

Task 5: Model Comparison and Domain Insights — use feature importance or permutation importance to identify the top 5 most predictive features. Explain what each implies in the real-world context of the problem.
preprocessing_requirements: Load the dataset containing audio recordings and corresponding emotion labels.
Extract and normalise MFCC features for each audio clip.
Check for class imbalance and handle it appropriately before splitting the data.
aiml_problems.expectedApproach: Use 2-3 specific ML algorithms such as Random Forest, Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP), and Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for training. Random Forest is chosen due to its robustness and ability to handle non-linear relationships without extensive tuning, while MLP provides a good balance between performance and interpretability for small datasets. CNN is suitable for sequence data and can capture temporal dependencies effectively.

It includes features extracted from the audio signals along with labels indicating whether the command was correctly recognized or not. The dataset aims to help researchers and engineers understand how environmental factors affect speech recognition performance.

LSTM is suitable for sequence data like time series or audio signals due to its ability to remember past information which helps in recognizing patterns over time.

Given the dataset containing 19 visual features extracted from image segments, the challenge is to build a robust model capable of distinguishing between five distinct outdoor scenes: sky, foliage, cement, window, and path. The dataset includes images labeled with these classes, providing a diverse range of scenarios for training and testing the model.
tasks: Task 1: Data Loading and Inspection — load the dataset. Display sample images from each class (at least 5 per class). Report the total number of images, image dimensions, color channels, and class distribution. Check for any corrupted or unusually sized images.

Task 3: Visual EDA — display a grid of sample images from each class. Plot the class distribution as a bar chart. For tabular metadata (if available), plot distributions of key attributes. Visualize mean and variance images per class.

Task 4: Model Training — train a baseline CNN and a pretrained model (e.g., MobileNetV2) with transfer learning. Evaluate using metrics appropriate for this target type. Plot a confusion matrix (classification) or residual plot (regression).

Task 5: Model Comparison and Domain Insights — use feature importance or permutation importance to identify the top 5 most predictive features. Explain what each implies in the real-world context of the problem.

aiml_problems.problemStatement: The challenge involves developing an AI system to classify brain tumor types based on Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) scans. Given a dataset containing 7023 MRI images divided into four categories—no tumor, glioma, meningioma, and pituitary tumor—the goal is to accurately diagnose brain tumors through automated analysis. This task is crucial for improving diagnostic efficiency and patient care in neurology departments.
tasks: Task 1: Data Loading and Inspection — load the dataset. Display sample images from each class (at least 5 per class). Report the total number of images, image dimensions (244x244 pixels), color channels (typically RGB), and class distribution. Check for any corrupted or unusually sized images.

Task 2: Image Preprocessing and Augmentation — resize all images to 244x244 pixels and normalise pixel values between 0 and 1. Apply data augmentation techniques such as random horizontal flip, rotation within ±15 degrees, and zoom within ±10%. Split the dataset into training (80%) and testing sets (20%) with stratified sampling to ensure balanced class representation. Report class balance after splitting.

Task 3: Visual EDA — display a grid of sample images from each class (no_tumor, glioma, meningioma, pituitary). Plot the class distribution as a bar chart. For tabular metadata if available, plot distributions of key attributes like age or gender. Visualise mean and variance images per class.

Task 4: Model Training — train a baseline CNN (such as MobileNetV2) and a pretrained model with transfer learning. Evaluate using metrics appropriate for classification (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, ROC-AUC). Plot a confusion matrix to understand misclassifications.

Task 5: Model Comparison and Domain Insights — use feature importance or permutation importance to identify the top 5 most predictive features. Explain what each implies in the real-world context of the problem, such as how certain MRIs characteristics might indicate the presence of different types of tumors.
preprocessing_requirements: Resize images to 244x244 pixels

Apply data augmentation including flips, rotations, and zooms

aiml_problems.expectedApproach:

2. **ResNet-50** - Pretrained on ImageNet, it provides strong initial feature extraction capabilities which can be fine-tuned for the specific task of brain tumor classification.

This dataset is commonly used for benchmarking text classification models due to its balanced class distribution and diverse content. aiml_problems.problemStatement: The problem at hand involves hyperparameter tuning for a computer vision application that aims to classify handwritten digits from the MNIST dataset. This dataset consists of 70,000 images of digits in 28x28 pixel grayscale format, representing 10 different classes (digits 0 through 9). The goal is to accurately predict the digit represented in each image using machine learning techniques while optimizing model parameters to achieve high accuracy and robustness across various classifiers.
tasks: Task 1: Data Loading and Exploration — load the dataset for 'Hyperparameter Tuning'. Display shape, dtypes, first 10 rows, and per-column missing value counts. Report descriptive statistics and identify any data quality issues specific to this domain (such as checking for zero variance features or outliers).

Task 2: Data Preprocessing — handle missing values (impute or drop as appropriate). Encode categorical features using LabelEncoder or OneHotEncoder as appropriate (Note: Since there are no explicit categorical features, this step may not be necessary here; focus on scaling the pixel intensity values using StandardScaler). Use stratified split for classification to ensure balanced representation of all classes in training and testing sets.

Task 3: Exploratory Data Analysis — Visualise the target distribution (digit frequencies). Plot the top-10 feature correlations with the target (which corresponds to the digit labels). Create 2–3 domain-specific plots relevant to 'Hyperparameter Tuning', such as visualizing the distribution of pixel intensities for each digit class or a histogram of digit frequencies.

Task 4: Model Training — Train at least 2 models of different families (e.g., one linear like Logistic Regression, one tree-based like Random Forest). Compare performance and justify which is more appropriate for this problem based on metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and AUC-ROC. Evaluate using metrics appropriate for this target type (classification) and plot a confusion matrix to visualize misclassifications.

Task 5: Model Comparison and Domain Insights — Use feature importance or permutation importance to identify the top 5 most predictive features. Explain what each implies in the real-world context of the problem (for example, certain edge detection features might contribute significantly to distinguishing between digits).
preprocessing_requirements: Scale the 784 features (28x28 flattened pixels) using StandardScaler to normalize the pixel intensity values before feeding them into the models.
Use stratified sampling during splitting to maintain class balance in both train and test datasets.
Check for and address any data quality issues such as zero variance features or outliers.
aiml_problems.expectedApproach: Logistic Regression and Random Forest Classifier. Logistic Regression can capture linear relationships effectively and provide interpretable coefficients, suitable for understanding feature contributions. Random Forest provides non-linear interactions and handles overfitting well due to its ensemble nature, making it a strong contender for hyperparameter tuning experiments.

aiml_problems.problemStatement: Given the 20 Newsgroups Text Classification dataset, the task is to develop a system capable of accurately categorizing news articles into one of 20 predefined categories such as 'comp.graphics', 'sci.med', 'talk.politics.government', etc., which can be useful for organizing online forums and news aggregators. This involves cleaning and preprocessing raw text data before applying machine learning models for classification.
tasks: Task 1: Data Loading and Text Inspection — load the dataset. Display 5 sample texts per class/label. Report vocabulary size, average token length, class distribution, and language distribution if multilingual. Identify any HTML artefacts, special characters, or encoding issues.

Task 2: Text Preprocessing — clean text (lowercase, remove punctuation/HTML/stopwords). Tokenise using an appropriate tokeniser (word-level or subword). Apply TF-IDF vectorisation or tokenise for a transformer model. Split 80/20 train/test with stratification on the label.

Task 3: Text EDA — plot word frequency distributions per class using bar charts or word clouds. Visualise text length distributions. Plot the class label distribution. Identify the most discriminative words using TF-IDF scores.

Task 4: Model Training — train a TF-IDF + Logistic Regression baseline and a TF-IDF + Random Forest model. If time allows, fine-tune a lightweight transformer like BERT. Evaluate using metrics appropriate for this target type. Plot a confusion matrix (classification) or residual plot (regression).

Task 5: Model Comparison and Domain Insights — Use feature importance or permutation importance to identify the top 5 most predictive features. Explain what each implies in the real-world context of the problem.
preprocessing_requirements: Lowercasing all text to ensure uniformity in processing.
Removing punctuation and HTML tags to cleanse the text data.
Tokenising the text into individual words or subwords for further analysis.
Stratified splitting to maintain the same proportion of labels in both training and testing sets.
aiml_problems.expectedApproach: For this dataset, we recommend starting with a TF-IDF + Logistic Regression model due to its simplicity and interpretability. It can help establish a strong baseline performance. Additionally, fine-tuning a lightweight transformer like BERT can capture more complex patterns in the text. The reasoning behind these choices is that logistic regression works well with sparse binary vectors produced by TF-IDF, while transformers excel at understanding contextual relationships within text.

aiml_problems.problemStatement: In high-energy physics experiments, distinguishing the presence of a Higgs boson from background processes is crucial for validating theoretical predictions and advancing our understanding of fundamental particles. This dataset contains kinematic features of particles produced in simulated particle collisions, where the goal is to classify whether a given event corresponds to the presence of a Higgs boson (signal) or not (background). The accurate detection of such rare events can significantly enhance our knowledge of subatomic interactions and improve the design of future collider experiments.
tasks: Task 1: Data Loading and Exploration — load the dataset for 'HIGGS Boson Detection'. Display shape, dtypes, first 10 rows, and per-column missing value counts. Report descriptive statistics and identify any data quality issues specific to this domain, such as outliers in kinematic features or imbalanced classes.

Task 2: Data Preprocessing — handle missing values (impute or drop as appropriate). Encode categorical features using LabelEncoder or OneHotEncoder as appropriate. Scale numerical features using StandardScaler or MinMaxScaler. Use stratified split for classification. Address class imbalance using SMOTE or class_weight='balanced'.

Task 3: Exploratory Data Analysis — Visualise the target distribution. Plot the top-10 feature correlations with the target. Create 2–3 domain-specific plots relevant to 'HIGGS Boson Detection', such as scatter plots of key kinematic features against the target or heatmaps of feature correlations.

Task 4: Model Training — Build a full ML pipeline using sklearn Pipeline or equivalent. Train at least 3 models including one ensemble (e.g., XGBoost or LightGBM) and one interpretable baseline (e.g., Logistic Regression). Perform hyperparameter tuning using GridSearchCV or Optuna. Use class_weight='balanced' or SMOTE to handle class imbalance. Evaluate with a full metric suite including accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, ROC-AUC, and confusion matrix. Plot learning curves to diagnose bias/variance. Use k-fold cross-validation (k=5) rather than a single train/test split.

Task 5: Model Comparison and Domain Insights — Use SHAP values to explain individual predictions. Identify the top drivers and explain their real-world meaning in the context of the problem domain, such as how certain kinematic features like transverse momentum or energy flow contribute to the detection of a Higgs boson.

Task 6: Model Deployment Readiness — export the best model using joblib or pickle. Write a predict() function that accepts raw input in the same format as the original features and returns a prediction with confidence score. Discuss what monitoring would be needed in production, such as regular retraining on new data, monitoring of feature drift, and continuous validation of model performance.
preprocessing_requirements: Imputing missing values in features like 'm_4l'
Encoding categorical features if present (though typically none in this dataset)
Scaling numerical features like 'pt_2' and 'eta_2'
aiml_problems.expectedApproach: For this dataset, suitable algorithms include XGBoost and LightGBM due to their effectiveness in handling large datasets and dealing with class imbalance. Additionally, a logistic regression model could serve as an interpretable baseline to compare against more complex models.

Task 3: Visual EDA — display a grid of sample images from each class. Plot the class distribution as a bar chart. For tabular metadata (if available), plot distributions of key attributes. Visualise mean and variance images per class.

Task 5: Model Comparison and Domain Insights — Use feature importance or permutation importance to identify the top 5 most predictive features. Explain what each implies in the real-world context of the problem.

aiml_problems.problemStatement: Develop an automated system to classify dermoscopy images of skin lesions into one of seven diagnostic categories (melanoma, melanocytic nevi, basal cell carcinoma, and others) to assist dermatologists in early detection and treatment planning of skin cancer. This system will leverage deep learning models trained on the HAM10000 dataset to provide accurate and timely diagnoses despite the presence of class imbalance in the real-world medical imaging data.
tasks: Task 1: Data Loading and Inspection — load the dataset. Display sample images from each class (at least 5 per class). Report the total number of images (10015), image dimensions (typically 224x224 pixels), colour channels (RGB), and class distribution. Check for any corrupted or unusually sized images.

Task 2: Image Preprocessing and Augmentation — resize all images to 224x224 pixels and normalise pixel values between 0 and 1. Apply data augmentation techniques such as random flip, rotation, and zoom to the training set only. Split the dataset into 80% training and 20% testing sets with stratified sampling to maintain class balance. Report class balance after splitting.

Task 3: Visual EDA — display a grid of sample images from each class. Plot the class distribution as a bar chart. For tabular metadata (if available), plot distributions of key attributes such as age, sex, etc., if present. Visualise mean and variance images per class to understand typical patterns within each category.

Task 4: Model Training — fine-tune a pretrained model like ResNet-50 using transfer learning. Freeze the base layers and train only the classification head first, then unfreeze and fine-tune the entire network. Evaluate the model with a comprehensive metric suite including accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC-ROC. Perform k-fold cross-validation (k=5) instead of a single train/test split to ensure robust performance across different subsets of the data. Plot learning curves to diagnose potential issues related to overfitting or underfitting.

Task 5: Model Comparison and Domain Insights — use SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values to explain individual predictions made by the model. Identify the top contributing factors and explain their significance in the context of diagnosing skin cancer. For example, certain textural features might indicate the likelihood of malignancy based on dermatological knowledge.

Task 6: Model Deployment Readiness — export the best performing model using joblib or pickle. Write a Python function named `predict()` that accepts raw input in the same format as the original features (such as resized and normalized images) and returns a predicted diagnosis along with a confidence score. Discuss what continuous monitoring mechanisms would be necessary in a production environment to ensure the model remains accurate and reliable over time.
preprocessing_requirements: Resize all images to 224x224 pixels
Normalise pixel values between 0 and 1
Apply data augmentation techniques to the training set (random flips, rotations, zooms)
aiml_problems.expectedApproach: Use transfer learning with pre-trained models such as ResNet-50 or EfficientNet-B0 due to their proven performance on similar image classification tasks. These models can handle the high-dimensional and complex nature of dermoscopy images while providing good generalization capabilities even when dealing with imbalanced classes.
